content.text:
*"Whatever you do, work at it with all your heart, as 
working for the Lord, not for human masters."*{Colossians 3:23}{A function is a correspondence between two sets that assigns to each element of the first exactly one element of the second.}{Peter Dirichlet}

A **function** is one of the most important concepts in all of 
mathematics. Functions describe relationships between quantities and 
provide the foundation for calculus, algebra, and computation.

content.title: Function
content.content:
A **function** $f$ from set $A$ to set $B$, written 
$f: A \to B$, is a rule that assigns to each element $a \in A$ exactly 
one element $b \in B$.

We call $A$ the **domain** and $B$ the **codomain**. 
If $f(a) = b$, we say $b$ is the **image** of $a$ under 
$f$, and $a$ is a **preimage** of $b$.

content.text:
The key requirement is *exactly one*: each input produces a unique 
output. A rule that gives different outputs for the same input is not a 
function.

content.title: Functions and non-functions
content.problem:
Let $A = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $B = \{a, b, c, d\}$.

**Function:** $f(1) = a$, $f(2) = a$, $f(3) = b$. Each input has 
exactly one output. (Multiple inputs can map to the same output.)

**Not a function:** A rule that assigns both $a$ and $b$ to input $1$. 
This violates the "exactly one" requirement.

**Not a function:** A rule defined only for $1$ and $2$ but not $3$. 
Every domain element must have an assigned value.

items: $f(a) = b$ to indicate that $a$ maps to $b$
$a \mapsto b$ (read "$a$ maps to $b$") in arrow notation

content.text: A function can be defined by:

content.title: Range
content.content:
The **range** (or image) of a function $f: A \to B$ is 
the set of all actual outputs:
\[ \text{range}(f) = \setbuild{f(a)}{a \in A} = \setbuild{b \in B}{\exists a \in A,\, f(a) = b} \]

content.text: The range is always a subset of the codomain, but they need not be equal.

content.title: Range vs. codomain
content.problem:
Let $f: \R \to \R$ be defined by $f(x) = x^2$.

The codomain is $\R$, but the range is $[0, \infty)$â€”only non-negative 
numbers are outputs of squaring.

content.title: Graph
content.content:
The **graph** of a function $f: A \to B$ 
is the set of all ordered pairs $(a, f(a))$:
\[ \text{graph}(f) = \setbuild{(a, f(a))}{a \in A} \subseteq A \times B \]

content.text:
The graph of a function passes the **vertical line test**: any 
vertical line intersects the graph at most once. This reflects the 
"exactly one output" requirement.

content.title: Function Equality
content.content:
Two functions $f: A \to B$ and $g: A \to B$ are **equal**, written 
$f = g$, if and only if $f(a) = g(a)$ for every $a \in A$.

content.text:
Functions are equal when they have the same domain, same codomain, and 
same input-output behavior.

content.title: Equal functions
content.problem:
Let $f(x) = (x+1)^2 - 2x - 1$ and $g(x) = x^2$ with domain $\R$.

Expanding: $f(x) = x^2 + 2x + 1 - 2x - 1 = x^2 = g(x)$ for all $x$.

Therefore $f = g$.

marginNotes.text:
Every good and perfect gift is from above, 
coming down from the Father of the heavenly lights, who does not change 
like shifting shadows.
